data.title: Boyd outlines $650M-$700M 2026 capex plan while reiterating $30M-$35M online EBITDAR guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Boyd Gaming (BYD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Keith Smith said Q1 results reflected “the benefits of our diversified business, our continued focus on operating efficiencies and our ongoing capital investment program,” highlighting company-wide revenues “nearly $1 billion” and EBITDAR of “$317 million,” with property margins “again exceeding 39%.”

  • CEO Smith described regional strength and Las Vegas headwinds, saying Midwest & South delivered “broad-based revenue and EBITDAR growth” (revenues up 4%, EBITDAR up 5%, margins “nearly 37%”), while Las Vegas Locals faced “continued softness in destination business” (largest impact at the Orleans) and “more significant construction disruption at the Suncoast,” with disruption expected to last “until we complete our renovation project late in the third quarter.”

  • CEO Smith pointed to new openings and pipeline projects, including “Cadence Crossing Casino on March 25,” a Suncoast modernization “on track for completion towards the end of the third quarter,” and a planned Orleans modernization project beginning in 2027. He also said the $750 million Virginia resort “remains on track for a late 2027 opening,” and that Boyd received “final approval from the Illinois Gaming Board” for Par-A-Dice, with the project expected to be completed “late 2028.”

  • CEO Smith emphasized shareholder returns, stating: “We returned nearly $170 million to our shareholders during the first quarter, $155 million in share repurchases and $14 million in dividends,” and added, “we intend to continue repurchases at a $150 million per quarter pace supplemented by our quarterly dividend.”

  • CFO Josh Hirsberg said: “During the quarter, we invested $155 million and expect to spend $650 million to $700 million in capital expenditures for the full year,” including “$300 million related to our Virginia project.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated online and managed segment full-year expectations, with CEO Smith saying, “we reiterate our previous guidance of $30 million to $35 million in EBITDAR for the Online segment this year,” and adding, “we continue to expect our Managed & Other business to generate $110 million to $114 million in EBITDAR for the full year.”

  • Management framed Las Vegas Locals pressure as driven by destination trends and Suncoast disruption timing. CFO Hirsberg said the destination headwind has been “about $5 million, $6 million of EBITDAR each quarter” since Q3 last year and added, “we’re expecting a similar impact in Q2,” with improvement expected to be “down less bad” later in the year and “maybe in the first half of next year, start to see some overall and growth out of that segment.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management language shifted from expecting Las Vegas Locals to “benefit” from Cadence Crossing and Suncoast completion in 2026 to more explicitly outlining Q2–Q3 pressures from “a full quarter in Q2 and a full quarter in Q3” of Suncoast disruption and a slower ramp to “full stride” at Cadence Crossing.

Financial results

  • CEO Smith reported Q1 company-wide revenues “nearly $1 billion,” EBITDAR of “$317 million,” and said “property margins again exceeding 39%,” attributing results to “broad-based strength in our Midwest and South segment,” partially offset by Las Vegas softness and Suncoast disruption.

  • CFO Hirsberg detailed capital allocation and balance sheet metrics, saying Boyd spent $155 million in capex in Q1 and expects “$650 million to $700 million” for 2026; Boyd repurchased “1.8 million shares at an average price of $83.94 per share,” ended Q1 with “74.8 million shares,” and has “approximately $700 million under our share repurchase authorization,” including “an additional $500 million authorized” earlier in April.

  • CFO Hirsberg said Boyd ended the quarter with “traditional leverage of 1.8x and lease-adjusted leverage of 2.4x,” and noted the company now expects to pay “the remaining $290 million during the second quarter” for tax credits related to the FanDuel transaction.

  • CFO Hirsberg flagged corporate expense as temporarily higher, stating, “there was about $6 million of onetime items,” with the “most prominent” related to “charitable contributions” timing.

Q&A

  • Steven Wieczynski, Stifel: asked when destination softness might inflect amid higher fuel prices; President & CEO Smith said it was “hard to kind of discern the impact of gas prices,” pointing to “easier comparisons” in the second half; CFO Hirsberg added the destination impact has been “about $5 million, $6 million of EBITDAR each quarter” and said recovery would likely be gradual.

  • Barry Jonas, Truist: asked why corporate expense was up and how to think about M&A; CFO Hirsberg cited “about $6 million of onetime items,” and President & CEO Smith said Boyd remains disciplined and would act “if the right opportunity presents itself.”

  • Shaun Kelley, BofA: questioned why Fremont traffic declined versus strip data and why locals lag regionals; President & CEO Smith confirmed “an 11% year-over-year decline” under the Fremont canopy and said he was “not overly concerned,” while adding “our pure locals business…is actually quite good.”

  • Benjamin Chaiken, Mizuho: pressed on bridging second-half Las Vegas moving parts and asked about expanding the Virginia temporary facility; CFO Hirsberg outlined Q3 pressures from destination and Suncoast and a Q4 setup with “Suncoast in the rearview mirror and Cadence hitting full stride,” while President & CEO Smith said there was “no ability to make this any larger” in Norfolk and that the temporary casino “is a breakeven.”

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked about intra-quarter cadence and cash taxes; President & CEO Smith cited weather/calendar dynamics and CFO Hirsberg said Suncoast disruption became most visible in March; on taxes, CFO Hirsberg said he was “currently estimating…about $45 million to $50 million” of cash tax benefit tied to accelerated depreciation timing.

  • James Hardiman, Citi: asked to quantify Suncoast disruption and whether Locals can grow; CFO Hirsberg said Las Vegas Locals EBITDAR was down “about $6.5 million,” attributing “probably $5 million to destination” and “about $1.5 million to Suncoast disruption,” and outlined Q2 and Q3 Suncoast impacts of “$2.5 million to $3 million” and “$2 million to $2.5 million,” respectively.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, repeatedly pressing on Las Vegas destination softness, construction disruption, and the likelihood of Las Vegas Locals growth, with framing such as “pretty significant headwind” (Steven Wieczynski, Stifel) and “hold out hope” concerns (James Hardiman, Citi).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing uncertainty and timing; CFO Hirsberg said, “I don’t think we expect it to flip on a dime,” while President & CEO Smith used qualifiers such as “hard to tell” on the destination inflection.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s posture moved from confidence that Suncoast disruption might be hard to discern to acknowledging, “we’re seeing it, and we’re letting you know” (CFO Hirsberg), while analysts stayed focused on the same central issue—destination softness centered on the Orleans.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Current quarter emphasized quantification and timing of Las Vegas pressures (destination impact and Suncoast disruption), while the prior quarter emphasized anticipated 2026 “benefit” drivers (Cadence opening, Suncoast completion, and tax legislation impacts) with less specificity on disruption magnitude.

  • In Q1, management added more concrete capital-return detail (repurchases “at a $150 million per quarter pace,” authorization “approximately $700 million,” and Q1 average repurchase price of $83.94) versus Q4’s emphasis on the ongoing pace without the updated authorization figure.

  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on Las Vegas destination softness, M&A posture, and iGaming optionality; Q1 questions leaned more into modeling mechanics for Q2–Q4 and quantifying specific drags (Suncoast and destination).

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited Las Vegas risks centered on “continued softness in destination business,” construction disruption that “will continue until…late in the third quarter,” and Downtown weakness tied to “an 11% year-over-year decline in pedestrian traffic” on the Fremont Street Experience.

  • CFO Hirsberg highlighted near-term cash uses and timing items, including the remaining “$290 million” tax credit payment expected in Q2 and corporate expense variability due to “onetime items.”

Final takeaway

Boyd management framed Q1 as a diversified, efficiency-driven quarter with Midwest & South growth offsetting Las Vegas destination softness and Suncoast disruption, while reaffirming key 2026 EBITDAR targets for Online ($30 million to $35 million) and Managed & Other ($110 million to $114 million). Management also highlighted a heavy 2026 investment plan of $650 million to $700 million in capex, continued share repurchases targeted at $150 million per quarter, and a clearer timeline for Las Vegas normalization tied to Suncoast completion in Q3 and a gradual easing of destination headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Medpace sees $1.94B backlog conversion over next 12 months as cancellations rise
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Medpace (MEDP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Quarter 1 of 2026 saw cancellations rise again with backlog cancels reaching their highest point in over a year,” said (Chairman & CEO August Troendle), adding, “Net bookings were below the level of -- seen in Q4… with a net book-to-bill ratio of 0.88.”

  • Troendle also flagged demand indicators and commercial execution priorities: “RFPs were down in the quarter sequentially and year-over-year. Initial award notifications and win rate were strong… we are focused on expanding our pipeline of opportunities and have implemented a number of initiatives to improve our win rate.”

  • (President Jesse Geiger) reported, “Revenue for the first quarter of 2026 was $706.6 million,” “Net new business awards entering backlog… increased… to $618.4 million,” and “Ending backlog… was approximately $2.9 billion,” while projecting, “approximately $1.94 billion of backlog will convert to revenue in the next 12 months.”

  • (CFO & Treasurer Kevin Brady) said, “EBITDA of $149.4 million… EBITDA margin for the first quarter was 21.1%,” and “In the first quarter, we generated $151.8 million in cash flow from operating activities,” while emphasizing, “Our 2026 guidance ranges for revenue, EBITDA, net income and EPS are unchanged from our prior quarter… There are no additional share repurchases in our guidance.”

  • Troendle announced a leadership change: “this will be our last earnings call with Jesse Geiger, our President,” and later said, “I will retake duties as President as I had in the past.”

Outlook

  • Brady reiterated 2026 framework changes were not made: “Our 2026 guidance ranges for revenue, EBITDA, net income and EPS are unchanged from our prior quarter based on an effective tax rate of 19% to 20% and interest income of $27.5 million. There are no additional share repurchases in our guidance.”

  • On durability despite cancellations, (Chairman & CEO Troendle) said, “our current modeling is we’re going to be within our guidance range on revenue,” while (CFO Brady) added, “Despite the headwind from cancellations… we feel very good about the range that we have out there.”

  • Compared with Q4 2025, Q1 2026 kept the same “no additional share repurchases” stance and maintained the full-year guidance ranges; the notable shift was Troendle’s acknowledgment that cancellations “continued” rather than easing.

Financial results

  • (President Geiger) reported “Revenue… $706.6 million,” “Net income per diluted share… $4.28,” and “backlog conversion in the first quarter was 23.3% of beginning backlog,” alongside “Ending backlog… approximately $2.9 billion.”

  • (CFO Brady) reported profitability and cash metrics: “EBITDA of $149.4 million,” “EBITDA margin… 21.1%,” “net income of $123.9 million,” “net days sales outstanding was negative 58.8 days,” and “we had $652.7 million in cash.”

  • On mix and expenses, Brady said margin stability reflected “higher reimbursable costs… offset primarily by lower employee-related costs,” and noted “Net income growth below EBITDA growth was primarily driven by a higher effective tax rate.”

Q&A

  • Max Smock, William Blair: asked if cancellations were macro-driven and whether themes were emerging; (Chairman & CEO Troendle) answered cancellations were “just the kind of random stuff you’d expect product performance, re-prioritizations,” and said it was “too early” to assess Q2.

  • David Windley, Jefferies: pressed on metabolic/GLP-1 volatility; Troendle responded, “metabolic has the lowest historically cancellation rate,” and said, “I just do not see the disruption… GLP-1… is actually a pretty safe therapeutic area for us.”

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked about gross bookings and M&A exposure; Troendle said, “we just report on net bookings,” and on M&A, “Clients of ours get acquired… we are cut out on future work. It’s a loss for us.”

  • Eric Coldwell, Baird: questioned long-dated backlog decline and future growth profile; Troendle conceded, “there is an area for concern,” adding, “our growth profile… in terms of sequential growth is a real question,” and said the remedy was “either cancellations to abate or gross awards… and a bigger pipeline.”

  • Jailendra Singh, Truist: asked about RFP weakness, competition, and AI; Troendle said he focuses on “quality of the opportunities,” stated, “I haven’t seen a large change” in competitiveness, and reiterated on AI, “the next 2 years at least, we’re going to be investing more dollars… than we will gain.”

  • Michael Cherny, Leerink: asked about buyback flexibility; (CFO Brady) said, “we’ve got a little bit over $800 million in authorization,” and “we’ll continue to execute.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on cancellations, backlog duration, and visibility, with Coldwell calling long-dated backlog decline “the elephant in the room” and Windley repeatedly probing metabolic/GLP-1 risk.

  • Management sentiment was neutral in prepared remarks but more defensive in Q&A, with Troendle stating, “I can’t dodge it and say, ‘Oh, yes, no, there’s no issue there,’” while also maintaining confidence via hiring: “We’re still hiring. I don’t know how you get any more confidence than that.”

  • Versus Q4 2025, the tone shifted from Troendle’s earlier “I see no reason to expect the higher level of cancellations to continue” to a more cautious posture in Q1 2026 as he said cancellations “frankly, hasn’t happened” to abate and sequential growth became “a real question.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 net book-to-bill was “0.88” versus Q4 2025 “1.04,” and Troendle described backlog cancellations as “highest… in over a year” in both quarters, but Q1 added that “RFPs were down… sequentially and year-over-year.”

  • Q1 management messaging placed greater emphasis on mitigation actions (“expand our pipeline… improve our win rate”) and acknowledged uncertainty further out (“’27 is… too far out”), while Q4 emphasized resilience (“book-to-bill rate does not need to be very high to generate growth”).

  • Analyst focus intensified from cancellations and pass-throughs in Q4 to deeper concerns in Q1 around beyond-12-month backlog coverage and the sustainability of sequential growth.

Risks and concerns

  • (Chairman & CEO Troendle) identified elevated cancellations as the central risk: “there is nothing we can do to alter our cancellation rate,” and later warned, “future cancellations could potentially impact” guidance.

  • Troendle highlighted demand-signal risk but downplayed its immediate usefulness: “RFPs were down… sequentially and year-over-year,” while adding, “the bouncing around of RFPs is overwhelmed by the quality of the RFPs.”

  • On client M&A, Troendle stated, “Clients of ours get acquired… we are cut out on future work,” framing it as recurring and negative for future work.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter where cancellations rose again and net bookings fell versus Q4, while still projecting approximately $1.94 billion of backlog converting to revenue over the next 12 months and reiterating 2026 guidance ranges (including a 19% to 20% effective tax rate assumption, $27.5 million of interest income, and no additional share repurchases). In Q&A, executives repeatedly tied near-term visibility and longer-dated backlog concerns to whether cancellations normalize and whether pipeline and win-rate initiatives translate into improved backlog formation over the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meritage projects Q2 2026 closings of 3,650-3,900 amid higher-than-anticipated incentives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Meritage Homes (MTH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Entering 2026, we are cautiously optimistic that lower interest rates and tenant demand will translate into a solid performance for homebuilders, balanced by more muted volatility," said Executive chairman Steven Hilton, while also citing disruption from "a severe winter storm" and that "military operations in Iran commenced at the end of February, increasing interest rates, price -- gas prices and inflation, all of which negatively impacted consumer confidence."

  • Hilton said Q1 sales orders were "3,664, 5% below last year's first quarter" and added that "capturing demand for the near term will require higher-than-anticipated use of incentives."

  • CEO Phillippe Lord said the company is leaning on community count rather than per-store velocity in the near term: "In the near term, we expect total volume and top line results will largely be driven by increased community count, not higher per store absorptions." He added that active communities rose to "345 by March 31, another company record," and reiterated "5% to 10% full year community count growth for 2026."

  • Lord said shareholder returns were increased despite the macro backdrop: "We intentionally stepped up our share buybacks repurchasing $130 million worth of common shares in Q1," and "we increased our dividend 12% to $0.48 per share."

  • CFO Hilla Sferruzza framed the quarter’s profitability pressure around incentives, land costs, and leverage: "Home closing gross margin of 17.5% for the quarter was 400 bps lower than prior year's 22% as a result of the increased use of incentives, higher lot costs and lost leverage."

Outlook

  • "Based on current market conditions, we are updating our guidance for full year 2026 on closing volume and revenue to at or within 5% of full year 2025 results," said CFO Hilla Sferruzza.

  • Sferruzza provided Q2 targets: "we are projecting total home closings between 3,650 and 3,900 units, home closing revenue of $1.37 billion to $1.47 billion, home closing gross margin around 18%, an effective tax rate of 24.5% to 25% and diluted EPS in the range of $1.18 to $1.46."

  • On what is driving near-term margin expectations, Lord pointed to competitive inventory and volume: "we see inventory levels stabilizing" and "we have the highest community count we've ever had," adding that even at "slower absorption paces, we think we can get there and not have to give up more margin to get there."

Financial results

  • CFO Hilla Sferruzza said Q1 "home closing revenue of $1.1 billion was 17% lower than prior year" and said results reflected "13% lower closing volume" and "a 5% decrease in ASP on closings" amid "a tougher demand environment this quarter."

  • Sferruzza said Q1 gross margin included discrete charges: "$2.4 million of real estate inventory impairment and $1.4 million in terminated land deal walkaway charges."

  • On cost offsets, Sferruzza said, "In Q1, we had direct cost savings of nearly 5% per square foot on a year-over-year basis," while also flagging inputs: "lumber costs have started to trend higher this quarter" and management was "monitoring any potential long-term inflationary impact on oil prices."

  • On liquidity and capital deployment, Sferruzza said the company ended the quarter with "cash of $767 million, nothing drawn under our credit facility and a net debt to cap of 17.4%," and added, "We returned $162 million of capital to shareholders via buybacks and dividends this quarter."

Q&A

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: "Are you seeing both your lower spec count and also kind of industry lower spec count ease the margin pressure here?" CEO Lord: "we saw across all of our markets, less finished inventory that we were competing with and we're optimistic as we move throughout the year, that creates a better environment for margin stability on a go-forward basis."

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked about off-balance-sheet exposure and walk-away flexibility; CFO Sferruzza: "about 38% of our total inventory control is off book" and "only about 10% of our total land supply is with traditional land bankers," adding, "we always have the ability...to walk away from that deal...So the only thing at risk for us would be the deposit and any other ancillary costs."

  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: asked if land-bank takedown extensions mathematically pressure margin; CEO Lord: "that was very small as well" and said it "really depends on the deal," while CFO Sferruzza added, "if you can't work a freebie, it's typically going to cost you whatever your interest burden is for that 6 months hold."

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman: asked if "18%" gross margin could be sustained; CEO Lord: "we see a couple of things" forming a floor, including "inventory levels stabilizing" and that the company can get volume from "the highest community count we've ever had."

  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: asked why Meritage sounded more cautious than peers; CEO Lord: "our buyer profile seems to be lacking the confidence" and "they're stretched more from an affordability standpoint," adding, "we're in the Sunbelt states primarily" where "affordability got the most stretched."

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP: asked about the West; CEO Lord: "we've been intentionally trying to reallocate a significant part of our business to the east of the West region" and "you'll continue to...see the West region be a smaller part of our business long term."

  • Jason Sabshon, KBW: asked for a mortgage-rate inflection point; CEO Lord: "as things sort of move out to [ 6 ] or slightly below [ 6 ]. We really see buyer psychology change below that level."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, focused on sustainability of margins, demand choppiness, and structural risks in land and regional performance, including questions on whether margin stability can hold and why Meritage is "more cautious" than peers.

  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks about strategy and capital return, but cautious on demand: Executive chairman Hilton said "capturing demand for the near term will require higher-than-anticipated use of incentives," and CEO Lord said "Consumer psychology remains fragile and can be driven by daily news announcements."

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management language shifted from early-year "encouraged by improved selling conditions in January" to emphasizing macro shocks and fragility in Q1 2026, while maintaining long-term confidence in the model and market-share opportunity.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1 2026, Executive chairman Hilton highlighted exogenous disruptions ("severe winter storm" and "military operations in Iran") while Q4 2025 framed the environment as "much softer-than-anticipated market conditions" tied to affordability and confidence.

  • The company’s demand and pricing posture stayed consistent across calls: in Q4 2025 CEO Lord said the company "intentionally elected to not further lean into incentives" in inelastic markets, and in Q1 2026 he reiterated, "we again committed to finding the right balance between velocity and margin."

  • Inventory and throughput continued tightening: in Q4 2025 management said it ended with "approximately 5,800 spec homes," while in Q1 2026 management said it ended with "approximately 4,700 spec homes" and positioned lower spec levels as aligned with current absorption targets.

Risks and concerns

  • Executive chairman Hilton cited confidence and rate shocks: "increasing interest rates" and "inflation" that "negatively impacted consumer confidence," adding that near-term demand capture needs "higher-than-anticipated use of incentives."

  • CFO Sferruzza flagged structural margin pressure from legacy land costs: "Our current land basis is primarily comprised of higher cost land vintages from 2022 through 2024 and will continue to negatively impact margins in 2026."

  • CEO Lord highlighted geographic and competitive pressure points: "Austin, parts of Florida and Charlotte continue to be tougher selling environment," and he described the entry-level buyer as "stretched more from an affordability standpoint."

Final takeaway

Management described a Q1 shaped by weather disruption, geopolitical-driven rate and inflation volatility, and fragile consumer psychology, with higher incentives pressuring margin. Against that backdrop, Meritage emphasized community-count-driven volume, a deliberately reduced spec position, and accelerated capital returns, while guiding Q2 closings of 3,650-3,900 and gross margin around 18% and reiterating that full-year 2026 closings and revenue should be at or within 5% of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lockheed Martin Aeronautics wins $177M contract modification for F-35 test jet
data.description:
• Lockheed Martin's (LMT) business area, Lockheed Martin Aeronautics, won a $177.5M contract modification for F-35 flight science aircraft support.
  • The contract covers building and delivering 3 new F-35 test jets (A, B, and C variants) with full engineering support for effective flight testing for Block 4 and future upgrades of the Joint Strike Fighter program.
  • The work is expected to be completed by April 2031.
  • At award, about $37.7M from Air Force, Navy R&D, and F-35 partners combined will be obligated and will expire by the end of the current fiscal year.
  • The contract is managed by Naval Air Systems Command.

data.title: RTX secures $369.9M contract modification for the F135 engine program
data.description:
• RTX (RTX) has secured a $369.9M modification to a fixed-price incentive contract for the F135 propulsion program.
  • The deal covers procurement of Lot 21 engines and long-lead materials, parts, and components, while also supporting delivery of 138 propulsion systems under Lot 20.
  • The work is expected to be completed by May 2031.
  • Around $157.2M in FY25–26 FMS funds will be awarded and will not expire this fiscal year.
  • The Naval Air Systems Command is the contracting authority.

data.title: Stewart anticipates 3%-5% residential market growth in 2026 while targeting low-teen RES margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stewart Information Services Corporation (STC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I am very pleased with the results of the first quarter this year" and "we delivered one of the best quarters in the company's history with adjusted EPS of $0.78 and revenue growth of 28%" (CEO & Director Frederick Eppinger).

  • "We expected existing home sales to improve around 6% to 8% in 2026" previously, but "we foresee the potential for growth to be a bit more muted this year" and "we could see the residential market continue to bounce along the bottom of around 4 million existing home sales" if geopolitical tensions persist (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • "We believe commercial on the other hand, will remain more resilient and continue to have solid growth" as Stewart cited commercial-driven strength across businesses, including "national commercial services" and "main street commercial" within direct operations (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • "In the first quarter, we added to our real estate solutions segment once more with the acquisition of National Appraisal Network into Stewart Valuation Intelligence" and said NAN will "help strengthen our appraisal growth scale and deepen our talent base" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • "Total first quarter revenues were $781 million, resulting in net income of $17 million or diluted earnings per share of $0.55" and "on an adjusted basis, net income was $24 million or diluted earnings per share of $0.78" (CFO & Treasurer David Hisey).

Outlook

  • "All in, our view of the residential market growth will be closer probably to 3% to 5% for the year" and "we do anticipate some momentum will continue into the second quarter" as rates are "at or below 2025 levels heading into the spring selling season" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • "We expect our title losses in 2026 to average in the 3.5% to 4% range" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

  • "In the first quarter, we delivered 12.5% adjusted margins, up from 9% last quarter" for Real Estate Solutions, and "for the full year, we fully expect to improve margins and deliver in the low-teen range for this segment" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Compared with the prior call’s tone on housing, management shifted from "we expected existing home sales to improve around 6% to 8% in 2026" to "closer probably to 3% to 5% for the year" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

Financial Results

  • "Total first quarter revenues were $781 million" and "net income [was] $17 million"; on an adjusted basis, "net income was $24 million" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

  • Title segment results were tied to commercial and agency activity: "operating revenues increased $104 million or 21%" and "title pretax income increased $13 million or over 100%"; on an adjusted basis, "adjusted pretax margin" was "4% compared to 2% last year" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

  • Commercial pricing/size metrics were a key driver: "Domestic commercial revenues increased $25 million or 35%" and "Average domestic commercial fee per file improved 33% to $21,000 compared to $15,800 last year" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

  • Agency and loss trends were framed as supportive: "gross agency revenues increased 25% to $333 million compared to $268 million last year," and "the first quarter title loss ratio improved to 3.1% compared to 3.5% last year" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

  • Balance sheet and cash flow commentary emphasized capacity: "Total cash and investments were approximately $420 million in excess of statutory premium requirements" and "net cash used by operations improved to $4 million compared to $30 million in the prior year quarter" (CFO & Treasurer Hisey).

Q&A

  • Bose George, KBW: asked whether elevated commercial fee-per-file can persist; CEO Eppinger said "our pipeline is really quite good," "the frequency of very large deals have increased," and "I'm pretty confident that the business will continue" though "the growth year-over-year will jump around" (CEO Eppinger).

  • Bose George, KBW: asked about Real Estate Solutions growth and margin outlook with MCS; CEO Eppinger said the segment is moving from "11% to 12%" to "12.5% to 13%, maybe a little 13% plus," and CFO Hisey added "MCS was a little over $160 million a year" (CEO Eppinger; CFO Hisey).

  • Geoffrey Dunn, Dowling: asked about agency commercial mix and RES margin composition; CEO Eppinger said agency commercial is "a pretty broad CRE kind of mix" and that the segment margin goal remains "around that 12% margin" with potential for "13%, 14% range" and, if volumes recover, "15% to 16%" (CEO Eppinger).

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about the National Appraisal Network deal and funding; CEO Eppinger said "it's about a $40 million thing" with "free cash on hand" used, referencing prior capital raising and "other dry powder" for additional deals (CEO Eppinger).

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about MCS integration and broader capital deployment; CEO Eppinger said "On the MCS, I'm thrilled" and that the business has "not a lot of integration" beyond "financial stuff," while describing acquisition capacity as "within the money we raised and probably we had $70 million on top of that available" (CEO Eppinger).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on sustainability of commercial strength ("could it persist for a little while?") and durability of RES margins post-acquisition (Bose George, KBW; Geoffrey Dunn, Dowling).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("I am very pleased") and confident on commercial ("I'm pretty bullish"), while more cautious on housing growth due to "broader macro and geopolitical conditions" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language on housing became more cautious, moving from "we expected" 6%-8% improvement to "closer probably to 3% to 5%" for 2026 (CEO & Director Eppinger).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Housing outlook language shifted: Q4 2025 framed "cautious optimism for housing in '26" and expectations to "begin to see modest market improvements in '26"; Q1 2026 added that growth could be "a bit more muted" and the market could "bounce along the bottom" near "4 million existing home sales" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Acquisition and deployment framing evolved from Q4 2025 "raising $140 million to provide additional dry powder" to Q1 2026 describing NAN as "about a $40 million thing" and indicating additional capacity as "within the money we raised" plus "$70 million on top" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Analyst focus remained consistent on commercial strength and fee-per-file dynamics, while the current quarter added more detailed questioning on RES margin composition and near-term integration execution (Bose George, KBW; Geoffrey Dunn, Dowling; Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens).

Risks and Concerns

  • "We foresee the potential for [housing] growth to be a bit more muted" and warned residential could "continue to bounce along the bottom" if "ongoing geopolitical tensions prolong" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Rate volatility was presented as a demand risk: March saw "rates exited the month around 6.3%, cooling activity a bit" due to "the quick shift in rate and sentiment" (CEO & Director Eppinger).

  • Integration/near-term costs were flagged for NAN: management cited "some integration costs and transition costs" and said "it's not big" financially (CEO & Director Eppinger).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a commercial-led quarter with higher profitability and a more cautious housing stance: CEO Frederick Eppinger highlighted "one of the best quarters" despite historically low residential activity, shifted the 2026 residential growth view to "3% to 5%," and reiterated that commercial should be "more resilient." CFO David Hisey underscored the quarter’s reported and adjusted earnings, improving title loss ratio, and liquidity, while Q&A centered on how durable commercial pricing/volume trends are, how quickly RES margins can move into the "low-teen" range, and how acquisitions like NAN and MCS fit into near-term capital deployment and integration priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Helen of Troy outlines FY27 net sales of $1.751B-$1.822B and adjusted EPS of $3.25-$3.75
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Helen of Troy (HELE) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “Net sales exceeded expectations and adjusted EPS was in line.” (CEO & Director George Uzzell)
  • “Overall sales trends reflect a volatile market. While our Home & Outdoor business held steady, our Beauty and Wellness business felt the pressure. The flu season didn't really happen.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • “Tariff mitigation was paramount, utilizing supplier diversification, SKU streamlining and pricing actions to protect our margins.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • “Debt reduction continues to be a priority, driven by strong free cash flow and a successful post-quarter divestment of our Southaven, Mississippi distribution facility.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • “We are winning where it counts... And we're taking market share.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • “Our fourth quarter results are a step in the right direction with net sales, adjusted EPS and cash flow at the better end of our expectations.” (Chief Financial Officer Brian Grass)
  • “For the full fiscal year, gross unmitigated tariffs had a $51 million impact on gross profit... we successfully reduced the net operating income impact to less than $30 million for the fiscal year.” (CFO Grass)

Outlook

  • “We expect net sales in the range of $1.751 billion to $1.822 billion... Adjusted EPS of $3.25 to $3.75 and free cash flow in the range of $85 million to $100 million.” (CFO Grass)
  • “We expect our quarterly sales cadence to be uneven... At the midpoint of our range, we expect first half year-over-year sales growth to be slightly positive with the second half of the year slightly negative.” (CFO Grass)
  • “We expect roughly 15% of our total annual adjusted EPS outlook in the first half of the year with roughly breakeven adjusted EPS in the first quarter.” (CFO Grass)
  • “We expect continued diversification of our global manufacturing footprint, reducing the cost of goods sold exposed to China tariffs to less than 20% by the end of fiscal '27 and limiting the net operating income impact to less than $10 million for the full fiscal year.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Our outlook reflects a deliberate choice to preserve investments in our brands and people and includes an increase in growth investments of approximately 40 basis points.” (CFO Grass)

Financial Results

  • “Consolidated sales decreased 3.3%.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Home & Outdoor segment sales declined 1.5%... OXO and Hydro Flask were ahead of plan and Osprey contributed solid year-over-year growth.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Beauty & Wellness sales decreased 4.7% with approximately 2.8 percentage points driven by tariff-related disruption.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Olive & June saw organic growth in its business of 18% and contributed 4.9 percentage points of growth to total segment sales.” (CFO Grass)
  • “International sales grew 5.4%.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Gross profit margin decreased 400 basis points to 44.6%.” (CFO Grass)
  • “Inventory ended at $456 million... Debt closed at $781 million... enabling $112 million of debt paydown in the quarter.” (CFO Grass)
  • “The sale generated proceeds of approximately $78 million, which we used to pay down our debt.” (CFO Grass)

Q&A

  • Peter Grom, UBS: “what success looks like on the other side of this?”; CEO Uzzell: “a healthy Helen of Troy is really about first being a better company before on our road to being a bigger company,” with priorities including “growth and market share,” “critical capabilities,” and “a healthy balance sheet.”
  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked about Iran-related volatility and guidance cadence; CFO Grass: “we haven't adjusted our outlook up or down as a result of the conflict,” and “the biggest part of it is the higher average tariff costs that are cycling out of our inventory into our cost of goods sold in the first half of this year.”
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities: asked how much pricing is embedded and retailer acceptance; CFO Grass: “it's about $50 million,” and “we have almost or effectively 100% of our planned pricing increases in place.”
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities: asked about increasing growth investment beyond 40 bps; CFO Grass: “we built the plan this year intentionally to lean into any over performance with additional growth investment.”
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity: asked brand drivers in Beauty & Wellness and Osprey trends; CFO Grass: “Olive & June and Revlon had relative strength,” wellness was “a little weaker,” and “the category is generally trending down, but Osprey is generally trending up and taking share.”
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity: asked about innovation and e-commerce investments; CEO Uzzell: “trying to pull innovation forward,” and “the bulk of my comments are really around digital capabilities... whether it be Meta Shop, TikTok Shop.”
  • Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James: asked why the company doesn’t expect 2H sales growth and category assumptions; CFO Grass: “We kind of assumed that current POS trends will continue... but we haven't assumed continued improvement,” and tariff recovery assumed is “about half.”
  • Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James: asked for early views on conflict-driven input costs; CFO Grass: “I can't give you any estimate of where that will go or end up.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, pressing on durability of recovery and conservatism in assumptions, including “what success looks like on the other side of this?” and “why do you not expect growth in the second half on sales?” (Peter Grom, UBS; Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James)
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on macro uncertainty, including “I am confident our teams are aligned,” alongside “we haven't adjusted our outlook up or down as a result of the conflict.” (CEO Uzzell; CFO Grass)
  • Versus the prior quarter, management repeated similar confidence framing around growth investment (“bias toward revenue improvement over aggressive cost reduction”) while analysts shifted toward deeper scrutiny of the multi-year path and the back-half weighting. (CFO Grass)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management formalized a multi-year framework: “a 3-phase evolution from stabilization to a portfolio of powerhouse brands. Fiscal '27 begins with Phase 1.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • In Q3, management signaled near-term planning and future disclosure: “FY '27 will be the first big step towards our future. More to come in the coming months.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • Pricing execution moved from partial to near-complete: Q4 noted “we have almost or effectively 100% of our planned pricing increases in place,” versus Q3 noting “less than full pricing realization” and “stop-shipments.” (CFO Grass)
  • Q4 added specific FY27 targets and cadence language (“breakeven adjusted EPS in the first quarter”), while Q3 focused on tightening FY26 ranges and tariff disruption narrative. (CFO Grass)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Overall sales trends reflect a volatile market... our Beauty and Wellness business felt the pressure.” (CEO Uzzell)
  • “Our fiscal '27 outlook includes tariffs in place as of April 2026 assumed to remain in effect... not including the benefit from any potential tariff refunds.” (CFO Grass)
  • “We continue to expect inflationary pressures, softness in discretionary categories, conservative retailer inventory management and an increasingly competitive and promotional landscape.” (CFO Grass)
  • “We have not attempted” to model Iran-conflict-linked commodity and freight swings; mitigation actions included buying raw material and contracting inbound freight. (CFO Grass)

Final Takeaway

Management positioned fiscal 2027 as a “turning point” anchored in a Phase 1 “restoration” plan, while outlining specific ranges for net sales, adjusted EPS, EBITDA, and free cash flow and emphasizing uneven quarterly cadence. The call highlighted tariff mitigation progress and further China-exposure reduction targets, near-complete pricing implementation (including about $50 million of price impact), and continued debt paydown supported by inventory reduction and a post-quarter facility sale, while acknowledging demand volatility (including a weak respiratory season) and the decision to prioritize brand and people investment over aggressive cost cutting amid uncertain macro conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Duke Energy gets NRC extension to operate Robinson nuclear plant to 2050
data.description:
Duke Energy (DUK) said post-market Thursday that it received a renewed operating license from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission for its Robinson nuclear plant in South Carolina for an additional 20 years, extending the plant's operations until 2050.

Robinson, located in Hartsville, S.C., delivers 759 MW of electricity to power nearly 570K homes; its original 40-year operating license was granted by the NRC in 1970, making it one of the first commercial nuclear power plants in the Southeast.

The Robinson plant is the second unit in Duke Energy's (DUK) six-site nuclear fleet to secure a subsequent license renewal, following the Oconee Nuclear Station in South Carolina in 2025; the company has said it plans to pursue the same extension for all 11 of its operating reactors across the Carolinas.

data.title: Univest targets 5% to 7% 2026 net interest income growth as it maintains 2% to 3% loan growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Univest Financial Corporation (UVSP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We had a strong start to the year as we reported net income for the first quarter of $27.1 million or $0.96 per share" and "our ROAA" was "1.33% for the quarter," while "increasing our quarterly dividend 4.5% to $0.23 per share" and "buying back 351,138 shares" (Chairman, President & CEO Jeff Schweitzer).

  • "We saw a solid NIM expansion during the quarter with reported NIM increasing 23 basis points to 3.33%" and "core NIM" was "3.44%" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Brian Richardson).

  • "Credit quality remained strong" with a "provision for credit losses of $1.3 million"; "nonperforming loans and leases" were "approximately 0.25% of total loans" and "net charge-offs" were "$1.3 million or 7 basis points annualized" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • "Noninterest income increased $1.7 million or 7.5%" year over year, and "when excluding BOLI death benefits, noninterest income increased $2.3 million or 11%"; management cited "continued strength in investment advisory, insurance and servicing-related fee income" and "increased risk participation and swap-related fee income" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • Expenses included "$427,000 of restructuring charges" and "an increase of $753,000 or 48.8% in medical claims expense"; Richardson said the self-insured medical plan "results in expense volatility based on the timing and magnitude of claims."

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our outlook for loan growth of approximately 2% to 3%, provisioning of $11 million to $13 million" and an effective tax rate of "20% to 21%" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • "We are updating our full year net interest income growth outlook to the range of 5% to 7%" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson). In the prior quarter, Richardson guided to "net interest income growth of approximately 4% to 6%."

  • Richardson reiterated rate-cut assumptions were not a swing factor: "whether there's 2 cuts or no cuts, we're kind of in the same range as the guidance that I provided."

Financial results

  • The quarter included "net income" of "$27.1 million" and "$0.96 per share" (Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer).

  • Margin and credit highlights included "reported NIM" of "3.33%" and an allowance for credit losses that "remained steady at 1.28% of loans held for investments" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • Capital actions cited in prepared remarks were a dividend increase to "$0.23 per share" and repurchases of "351,138 shares" (Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer).

Q&A

  • Jacob Morton, Stephens: asked about deposit cost spot rates and further room to reduce; Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson said "we're starting to get to a little bit of a point of equilibrium," with spot cost "down 10 basis points" from "12/31 to 3/31" and "relative stability" expected.

  • Morton, Stephens: asked about the drop in cash balances and deployment timing; Richardson attributed it to "seasonality" and "runoff of public funds," adding "we normally hit the trough at the end of the second quarter" tied to Pennsylvania tax cycles.

  • Morton, Stephens: asked about pipeline, growth expectations, competition, and incremental yields; President & Director Mike Keim said the "pipeline is solid" and highlighted "normalization of our prepayment activity," while noting CRE is "more competitive" but "construction" margins are "still strong." Richardson said new commercial pricing was in the "mid-6 range."

  • Emily Lee, KBW: asked about rate-cut assumptions and NIM sensitivity; Richardson said initial guidance assumed "2 rate cuts" and that modest changes were "not overly impactful" to near-term NII/NIM.

  • Lee, KBW: asked about buybacks and capital deployment; Schweitzer said, "I don't anticipate us pulling back on buybacks," while Richardson said CET1 was "11.22%" entering the year and "11.32%" at quarter-end, adding, "we actually look to ratchet that back down to that 11.22% or lower range here" and "ramping up buybacks accordingly."

  • Lee, KBW: asked about capital priorities and M&A; Schweitzer said, "the best use of our capital appears to be on buying back shares," while remaining "open to looking at M&A opportunities" across "bank," "wealth," and "insurance" and "more so than we probably were the last few years."

  • Lee, KBW: asked about credit watch items; Schweitzer said, "there's no trends that we're seeing in our portfolio that are concerning" and pointed to monitoring "higher fuel costs and energy costs" and fertilizer affordability in the ag portfolio.

  • Lee, KBW: asked about loan repricing mix; Richardson said, "About 1/3 of the book is purely floating, about 30% is fixed" with the remainder adjustable.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive to positive, with questions concentrated on deposit costs, NIM/rate sensitivity, capital deployment, and competitive loan pricing; one investor-commenter said it was "just a terrific quarter" (Chris Reynolds, Neuberger Berman).

  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and consistent in Q&A, emphasizing stability and execution, including "We had a strong start to the year" (Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer) and guidance language such as "We are maintaining our outlook" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted toward stronger momentum on margin and NII growth (Q1: "updating" NII growth to "5% to 7%" vs. Q4: "4% to 6%"), while analyst focus stayed anchored on deposits/liquidity seasonality and competition.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Prepared-results momentum: Q1 2026 included "$27.1 million" net income and "$0.96 per share" (Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer) vs. Q4 2025 "$22.7 million" and "$0.79 per share" (Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer).

  • Margin narrative reversed: Q4 2025 described "a slight compression" in reported NIM to "3.10%" due to excess liquidity (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson), while Q1 2026 reported NIM rose to "3.33%" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson).

  • Capital return emphasis strengthened: Q4 targeted buybacks of "$10 million to $12 million per quarter" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson), while Q1 reiterated no pullback and tied pace to a CET1 target around "11.22%" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Richardson; Chairman, President & CEO Schweitzer).

Risks and concerns

  • Funding-cost upside appeared limited: Richardson said deposit costs are near "a little bit of a point of equilibrium" and deposit growth needed to lower the loan-to-deposit ratio "puts a little bit of pressure on cost of funds."

  • Competitive loan pricing risk was discussed as "more competitive" on CRE and "strong C&I credits" (President & Director Keim), with Univest emphasizing niches such as construction where "margins are still strong."

  • Expense volatility was flagged from the self-insured medical plan: Richardson said it "results in expense volatility" and Q1 included a "48.8%" jump in medical claims expense.

  • Credit monitoring centered on borrower pass-through and ag input costs; Schweitzer said some shipping/distribution customers are "putting surcharges in" and fertilizer impacts may be "a next year consideration."

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a strong earnings and margin quarter, paired with stepped-up capital return through a higher dividend and continued buybacks, while holding core growth and credit-cost expectations steady. The key forward-looking change was Richardson’s update to full-year 2026 net interest income growth of 5% to 7%, alongside commentary that deposit costs are approaching equilibrium and that competitive lending conditions have intensified in CRE and top-tier C&I, with Univest emphasizing construction and other niches to maintain pricing discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
